26: A direct revelation: 'For as often as you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes.' The Eucharist is not merely commemorative but kerygmatic — every celebration is a proclamation (kataggellō) of the Lord's death. This death-proclamation has an eschatological horizon: 'until he comes' (achris hou elthe). The Lord's Supper situates the community between the cross and the parousia, making every meal a re-entry into the narrative of Christ's saving death and an anticipation of its completion at the return. Christology is embedded in liturgical practice.
1: 'Be imitators of me, as I am of Christ.' The chain of imitation is explicitly Christological: believers imitate Paul; Paul imitates Christ. The apostle's kenotic mission-pattern (1 Corinthians 9), his voluntary limitation of rights, his 'becoming all things to all people' for the gospel — all of these are not original with Paul but derived from the pattern of the incarnate Son who 'came not to be served but to serve' (Mark 10:45). Christological discipleship is mimetic: transformation into the image of Christ by following those who have been transformed.
2: 'Now I commend you because you remember me in everything and maintain the traditions even as I delivered them to you.' The apostolic traditions (paradoseis) that Paul delivered are the same vocabulary as the reception-and-transmission of the resurrection tradition (1 Corinthians 15:3) and the Lord's Supper institution (1 Corinthians 11:23). These traditions are not Paul's inventions but the deposit of the Lord's own words and actions, transmitted through the apostolic chain. The Christ-community is defined by its fidelity to this transmitted tradition.
3: 'The head of every man is Christ, the head of a wife is her husband, and the head of God is Christ.' The Christological claim here is the foundation of the entire head-covering discussion: Christ is the kephalē (head) of every man. Headship in Pauline usage carries the sense of source, authority, and representative relationship — not domination but ordered, loving authority-with-responsibility. That Christ is head of 'every man' (not just believing men) extends his lordship to all humanity, though it is recognized only within the covenant community.
4: A man who prays or prophesies with his head covered 'dishonors his head.' The covering of the head that was an act of honor in Roman religious rites (the capite velato posture of the Roman pater familias at sacrifice) is reinterpreted in light of Christ's headship: for a man to veil his head in worship obscures the Christological glory that man as the image of Christ is to display. The creation-order argument that Paul develops in 1 Corinthians 11:7-9 is rooted in man's relationship to Christ as his head.
5: A woman who prays or prophesies with her head uncovered 'dishonors her head.' The Corinthian context in which women prophesied (clearly assumed to be legitimate, not prohibited here) is significant: women in the new covenant community participated in vocal, public ministry before the congregation. The head covering for women is a sign of the creation order that contextualizes this Spirit-given ministry within the structure of covenantal relationship — affirming the ministry while situating it within the order Christ has established.
6: 'For if a wife will not cover her head, then she should cut her hair short. But since it is disgraceful for a wife to cut her hair short or shave her head, let her cover her head.' The reductio ad absurdum argument: if covering the head is unnecessary, then the cultural marker of femininity (long hair) is equally unnecessary. The logic assumes that the social order communicating gender distinction and proper covenantal relationship is not arbitrary but reflects the created order that Christ's redeeming work restores and fulfills, not obliterates.
7: 'For a man ought not to cover his head, since he is the image and glory of God.' Man as the image of God (eikōn kai doxa Theou) is the Genesis 1:26-27 basis for the head-covering argument. Christ, as the true image of God (Colossians 1:15; 2 Corinthians 4:4), is the archetype from which Adam's image-bearing derived its meaning. In worship, the man is to display this image-glory uncovered — reflecting the God whose image he bears. The created order is a Christological testimony: it was always pointing toward the one in whose image humanity was made.
8: 'For man was not made from woman, but woman from man.' The Genesis 2 creation account — Adam formed first, Eve drawn from Adam — provides the created-order basis for the relational structure Paul is defending. The Christological application: just as woman's origin was from man, the church's origin is from Christ. The structural relationship of source-and-derived is not a hierarchy of value but of covenant order: the one who originates defines the relationship for the one who derives from him.
9: 'Neither was man created for woman, but woman for man.' The purposive statement from Genesis 2:18 ('it is not good for the man to be alone; I will make him a helper fit for him') grounds the relational ordering in God's creational purpose. The Christological parallel: the church was created for Christ — to be his body, his bride, his community of glory. Creation's purpose-structures are covenantal: the design reveals the relationship, and both testify to the Christ who was always their goal.
10: 'That is why a wife ought to have a symbol of authority on her head, because of the angels.' The mysterious 'because of the angels' (dia tous angelous) has generated extensive interpretation. The most plausible background is the Jewish belief that angels attend worship and observe the order of God's covenant community (cf. 1QSa 1 Corinthians 2:8-9: the angels present in the assembly; Psalms 138:1 LXX). The ordered worship of the church is a performance before cosmic witnesses — the angels who observe whether the community reflects the covenant order established in Christ.
11: 'Nevertheless, in the Lord woman is not independent of man nor man of woman.' The qualification is crucial: 'in the Lord' (en kyriō) — the christological sphere — the mutuality and interdependence of man and woman are fully affirmed. The creation-order argument does not establish male superiority but relational structure within mutual dependence. The new community in Christ is not a hierarchy of value but an ordered community of mutual need and mutual gift, where each serves the other's good.
12: 'For as woman was made from man, so man is now born of woman. And all things are from God.' The circular mutual dependence — woman from man (creation), man from woman (birth) — and the ultimate divine source ('all things are from God') roots the entire creation-order argument in Christological theology. God, as creator and redeemer, is the source of all relational structure. The one through whom all things were created (1 Corinthians 8:6) has also ordered the covenantal relationships within which the church worships and reflects his glory.
13: 'Judge for yourselves: is it proper for a wife to pray to God with her head uncovered?' The appeal to the community's own judgment about propriety in worship echoes the wisdom appeal of 1 Corinthians 10:15. The Corinthians who prided themselves on spiritual discernment are invited to exercise that discernment about worship order. The question assumes a normative order of worship in which contextual appropriateness is not arbitrary but reflects the created and covenantal structures that Christ has established and redeemed.
14: 'Does not nature itself teach you that if a man wears long hair it is a disgrace for him?' The 'nature' (physis) argument — drawing on observable patterns of creation — was a standard Stoic and Jewish method of moral reasoning from the created order. Paul's use of natural observation alongside scriptural argument reflects the Reformed understanding of general revelation: creation itself testifies to God's design. The Christological underpinning is that creation reflects the creator whose image-structures are being displayed and reflected in ordered worship.
15: 'But if a woman has long hair, it is her glory? For her hair is given to her for a covering.' The natural hair-covering given to women as 'glory' (doxa) echoes the 'glory' language of 1 Corinthians 11:7-8: man is the glory of God; woman is the glory of man; woman's hair is her glory. The creation gifts that express relational identity — the hair covering given by nature — are not accidental but embedded in the created order. The God who created these natural expressions of relational identity is the same God who establishes covenant worship order in Christ.
16: 'If anyone is inclined to be contentious, we have no such custom, nor do the churches of God.' The apostolic appeal to universal church practice is a significant hermeneutical move: the ordering of worship is not a local Pauline preference but the common practice of all the churches of God. The Christological logic: the church universal, formed by the one gospel and the one Spirit, exhibits consistent patterns of worship that reflect the one Lord's ordering of his community. Contentious deviation from this common practice is not freedom but disorder.
17: 'But in the following instructions I do not commend you, because when you come together it is not for the better but for the worse.' The Lord's Supper section begins with the most direct rebuke in the letter: the Corinthians' eucharistic practice is actually counter-productive — their gatherings make things worse rather than better. The meal that should be the highest expression of Christ-community (the body of Christ sharing in the body of Christ) has become a site of division, shame, and social hierarchy — the opposite of what the Lord's death proclaimed (1 Corinthians 11:26).
18: 'For, in the first place, when you come together as a church, I hear that there are divisions among you.' The divisions (schismata) in the Corinthian gathering — the same word as 1 Corinthians 1:10 — now manifest at the Lord's Supper table, the one place where unity should be most visible. The social stratification of Corinthian society (wealthy members bringing lavish meals, poor members bringing little or nothing) is being replicated at the table of the one who 'though he was rich became poor' for them (2 Corinthians 8:9). The eucharist is being un-christologized by class distinction.
19: 'For there must be factions among you in order that those who are genuine among you may be recognized.' The 'necessity' (dei) of factions echoes apocalyptic language about the eschatological testing that reveals genuine faith. God's providential ordering of even the church's failures serves the purpose of making visible who truly belongs to Christ. The genuine (dokimoi) are revealed through the crisis — those who, even in the disordered Corinthian situation, preserve the eucharistic meaning by waiting for one another and honoring the poor.
20: 'When you come together, it is not the Lord's Supper that you eat.' The devastating verdict: what the Corinthians are doing when they 'come together' does not constitute the Lord's Supper. The Lord's Supper requires that the death of the Lord be proclaimed (1 Corinthians 11:26) — but a meal where the wealthy eat their fill while the poor go hungry proclaims not the Lord's self-giving but the world's self-keeping. The form of the meal has been evacuated of its Christological content by the social practice surrounding it.
21: 'For in eating, each one goes ahead with his own meal. One goes hungry, another gets drunk.' The specific abuse: the wealthy arrived early and ate their privately-brought food (Corinthian practice at communal meals was to bring food proportional to one's status), while the working poor who arrived late found nothing and watched the wealthy drunk. The class-stratified Roman meal custom, imported into the Christian gathering, contradicted the Lord's Supper's essential declaration that the one body of Christ shares in the one sacrifice equally.
22: 'What! Do you not have houses to eat and drink in? Or do you despise the church of God and humiliate those who have nothing?' Paul's question exposes the theological stakes: treating the poor with contempt at the Lord's table is despising the church of God — the community bought by Christ's blood (1 Corinthians 6:20; 1 Corinthians 7:23). The poor in the Corinthian community are members of the body of Christ (1 Corinthians 12:12-27); shaming them at the eucharist is shaming Christ himself in the members of his body.
23: 'For I received from the Lord what I also delivered to you, that the Lord Jesus on the night when he was betrayed took bread.' The institution narrative is introduced as something 'received from the Lord' — apostolic tradition with the highest possible authority. The specific detail 'on the night when he was betrayed' (en tē nykti hē paredideto) grounds the eucharist in the concrete historical moment of the passion. The meal is not a timeless spiritual ritual but a specific memorial of a specific night when the covenant Lord was handed over for the sins of his people.
24: 'This is my body which is for you. Do this in remembrance of me.' The institution of the bread-as-body contains the self-interpretive word of the Lord: 'for you' (hyper hymōn) — the substitutionary/representative character of his death. The 'remembrance' (anamnēsis) is not mere mental recollection but covenantal re-presentation — the memorial that makes present the saving event it recalls, as the Passover Seder re-presented the exodus for each generation. Every eucharist is a participation in the once-for-all sacrifice 'for you.'
25: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood. Do this, as often as you drink it, in remembrance of me.' The new covenant (kainē diathēkē) in Christ's blood fulfills and surpasses the Mosaic covenant ratified by the blood of bulls and goats (Exodus 24:8). Jeremiah 31:31-34 promised a new covenant with different terms: not written on stone but on hearts; not requiring daily sacrifice but with sins forgiven once-for-all. The cup of the Lord's Supper is the enacted proclamation that this new covenant has been inaugurated in Christ's death — the cup of the new age.
27: 'Whoever, therefore, eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty concerning the body and blood of the Lord.' The high Christological stakes of eucharistic participation: unworthy eating is not disrespect for a ritual but participation in the guilt of those who crucified the Lord. The body and blood of Christ present at the table make the table a place where the death and resurrection of Christ are enacted — to participate carelessly is to join the ranks of those who dismissed the Son of God. The anamnesis makes the past event contemporaneous with the present community.
28: 'Let a person examine himself, then, and so eat of the bread and drink of the cup.' The self-examination required before eucharistic participation is the Christological discipline of discernment: does this eating-and-drinking proclaim the Lord's death, or does it contradict it? The examination is not perfectionist self-scrutiny but orientation-check: am I approaching this table as one who depends on Christ's body given for me, or am I treating it as a social occasion? The sacrament requires a consistent Christological consciousness.
29: 'For anyone who eats and drinks without discerning the body eats and drinks judgment on himself.' 'Discerning the body' (diakrinōn to sōma) most likely refers both to the eucharistic body of Christ (present in the bread) and the ecclesial body of Christ (present in the gathered poor who are being shamed). Failing to discern the body of Christ in both its dimensions — the sacramental and the social — brings divine judgment. The Christological unity of the eucharistic and ecclesial body is the ground of Paul's entire argument in this section.
30: 'That is why many of you are weak and ill, and some have died.' The physical consequences (weakness, illness, death) are divine judgment on eucharistic abuse. Paul interprets the community's health crises in a Christological frame: the Lord who disciplines those he loves (Hebrews 12:6; Proverbs 3:12) visits judgment on those who desecrate the body and blood that are the very means of their salvation. The judgment is not retributive destruction but corrective discipline, as 1 Corinthians 11:32 will clarify: 'judged by the Lord so that we may not be condemned along with the world.'
31: 'But if we judged ourselves truly, we would not be judged.' The self-examination called for in 1 Corinthians 11:28 is the preventive form of the judgment that God otherwise imposes. The Christological logic: Christ has already borne the ultimate judgment for sin on the cross; the Christian who appropriately applies that judgment to himself ('yes, I am a sinner who needs this sacrifice') is already participating in the cross's verdict and needs no additional judgment. Self-examination is the individual application of the cross's logic.
32: 'But when we are judged by the Lord, we are disciplined so that we may not be condemned along with the world.' The distinction between divine discipline and eschatological condemnation is crucial: the illness and death of 1 Corinthians 11:30 are not signs of divine rejection but of fatherly correction (cf. Hebrews 12:7-11). The goal of the discipline is inclusion in the final salvation, not exclusion. Christ who bore the condemnation of the world (Romans 8:3) ensures that those who belong to him receive discipline rather than condemnation — even severe discipline serves their ultimate good.
33: 'So then, my brothers, when you come together to eat, wait for one another.' The practical Christological application: the cure for the eucharistic disorder is waiting — waiting as the most basic act of mutual regard. The wealthy who arrive early and eat their fill before the poor arrive must wait. Waiting for one another at the Lord's table enacts the Christological principle that all members of the body are equally dependent on the one who died for them. The meal that proclaims the Lord's death must embody the Lord's death in its practice of self-giving.
34: 'If anyone is hungry, let him eat at home, so that when you come together it will not be for judgment.' The Lord's Supper is not a meal for satisfying hunger — that is what homes are for. The gathering of the church is specifically for the proclamation of the Lord's death (1 Corinthians 11:26), for sharing in his body and blood (1 Corinthians 10:16), and for forming the one body of Christ (1 Corinthians 10:17). Separating the eucharistic meal from ordinary social dining preserves the Christological meaning of what the church does when it gathers — it gathers around the crucified and risen Lord, not around social status.
